title: Object and function types should be explicitly stated in their declarations and definitions
description: Object and function types should be explicitly stated in their declarations and definitionsAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearmaintainabilityCode Smellbased-on-misracertWhy is this an issue?More InfoThe C90 standard allows implicit typing of variables and functions, and some C compilers still support legacy code by allowing implicit typing. But it should not be used for new code because it might lead to confusion.Noncompliant code exampleextern x; const x; static fun(void); typedef ( *pfi ) ( void );Compliant solutionextern int16_t x; const int16_t x; static int16_t fun(void); typedef int16_t ( *pfi ) ( void );Available In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab repositoriesAnalyze code in youron-premise CIDeveloper
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
extern x; // Noncompliant: implicit int type
const y; // Noncompliant: implicit int type
static fun(void); // Noncompliant: implicit return type
typedef (*pfi)(void); // Noncompliant: implicit return type

global_var = 42; // Noncompliant: no type declaration
function() { return 0; } // Noncompliant: no return type
compliant:
extern int x; // Compliant: explicit int type
const int y; // Compliant: explicit int type
static int fun(void); // Compliant: explicit return type
typedef int (*pfi)(void); // Compliant: explicit return type

int global_var = 42; // Compliant: explicit type
int function(void) { return 0; } // Compliant: explicit return type

checks.message: Object and function types should be explicitly stated in their declarations and definitions. Add explicit type specifiers (int, char, void, etc.) to improve code clarity.
